title: Getting random 403 Forbidden errors on the dashboard
body: I keep getting 403 errors when I try to access the TaskFlow dashboard. It's not every time — sometimes it loads fine, sometimes I get a 403 page. It seems totally random. My permissions haven't changed and other people on my team say they're seeing it too. Started happening a couple days ago.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — intermittent 403s are frustrating, especially when permissions haven't changed.

To help us narrow this down: when you get the 403, is it when you first load the dashboard, or does it happen on specific actions after the page is already loaded (like switching tabs, filtering tasks, etc.)? And if you refresh the page after getting a 403, does it load successfully right away, or does it take several attempts?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It happens right when I try to load the dashboard — I get a 403 before anything even shows up. And yeah, if I just hit refresh it usually works on the next try, or maybe the one after that. It's weird, it almost feels like a network issue or something. Like every few requests it just decides I don't have permission.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That refresh behavior is a really useful clue — it points away from a real permissions problem and toward something intermittent in the infrastructure.

When you get the 403, what does the error page actually look like? Specifically: is it TaskFlow's styled error page (with the app's branding/layout), or is it a plain/generic error page that looks different from the rest of the app? If you're comfortable checking, the browser's developer tools (Network tab) could also tell us whether the 403 is coming from the TaskFlow API itself or from something in front of it (like a CDN or proxy) — the response headers or body would help a lot here.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Hmm, I'm not really sure how to check the developer tools thing, sorry. But the error page — I think it's the regular TaskFlow one? It has the logo at the top and says something like 'You don't have permission to access this page.' It doesn't look like a totally different page or anything. It looks the same as when I accidentally tried to access the admin settings once before I had the right role.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful — knowing it's TaskFlow's own error page tells us the app itself is making the permission decision, which narrows things down a lot.

I want to challenge the idea that it's truly random: have you noticed any pattern at all in when it happens? For example, does it tend to happen more when you first log in for the day, or after you've been away from the app for a while? Or does it happen just as often if you're actively using TaskFlow and navigate back to the dashboard? Basically — does the 403 feel connected to how long it's been since you last used the app, or does it really seem to strike at any time regardless?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: No, it doesn't seem connected to timing at all. It happens just as much when I'm actively clicking around as when I first open it in the morning. Honestly it feels like it's roughly every third time I try? Like I'll get in fine, then fine again, then 403, then fine, fine, 403... it's not exactly that predictable but it does feel like it fails maybe a third of the time, give or take. I don't think it has anything to do with sessions or being logged out or anything like that.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That 'roughly every third time' pattern is actually a strong clue — it doesn't sound random at all, it sounds like a regular cycle.

Do you know if there were any deployments, updates, or infrastructure changes to TaskFlow around the time this started a couple days ago? Even a routine update or maintenance window could be relevant. And if you happen to know whether your team uses TaskFlow behind a load balancer (i.e., multiple servers handling requests), that would be very helpful — though I understand that might be more of a question for whoever manages the infrastructure on your side.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I think there was some kind of update or deployment a couple days ago, yeah — around the same time this started. I remember getting an email about a new 'analyst' role being added, which is actually the role I was just assigned to. Not sure if that's related? As for the load balancer thing, I honestly don't know the details of how it's set up on the infrastructure side. I know we have multiple servers but I couldn't tell you specifics — that'd be a question for our IT team.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That timing with the new 'analyst' role is really interesting — and potentially the key to this whole thing.

Quick question: the other people on your team who are also seeing these 403 errors — are they also on the new 'analyst' role? And do you know if anyone on a different role (like a regular member or admin) has been seeing the same issue, or is it just people with 'analyst'?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question — yeah, I think the people on my team who mentioned it are also analysts. We all got switched over to the new analyst role around the same time. I haven't heard complaints from anyone who's an admin or editor, but I also haven't specifically asked around. It does seem like it might just be us analysts though, now that you mention it.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent 403 Forbidden for users with new 'analyst' role — likely inconsistent deployment across backend servers","problem":"Users assigned the newly created 'analyst' role receive 403 Forbidden errors approximately one-third of the time when loading the dashboard. Refreshing usually succeeds on the next attempt. The issue began immediately after a deployment that introduced the analyst role. Only analyst-role users appear affected.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The deployment that added the 'analyst' role was not applied consistently across all backend servers behind the load balancer. One out of approximately three servers is missing the analyst role definition (or has a stale permission/policy configuration), so requests routed to that server fail authorization while the other servers handle them correctly.","reproduction_steps":["Assign a user the 'analyst' role","Attempt to load the TaskFlow dashboard repeatedly (10+ times)","Observe that roughly 1 in 3 requests returns a 403 Forbidden (TaskFlow's own branded error page)","Refresh after a 403 — subsequent request typically succeeds"],"environment":"Multi-server deployment behind a load balancer. A deployment introducing the 'analyst' role occurred approximately 2 days before the report. Specific infrastructure details (LB type, server count) not confirmed but the ~33% failure rate suggests 3 backend instances with 1 misconfigured.","severity":"high","impact":"All users with the 'analyst' role are affected. They cannot reliably access the dashboard, requiring repeated refreshes. This is a new role with multiple team members recently migrated to it, so the affected population is likely growing.","recommended_fix":"1. Verify deployment status across all backend servers — check that the analyst role definition and its associated permissions exist on every instance. 2. Compare the authorization/RBAC configuration (database records, config files, or policy definitions) across servers to find the inconsistency. 3. Redeploy or sync the missing role configuration to the out-of-date server(s). 4. Consider adding a health check or deployment verification step that validates all defined roles are present on each server before marking a deployment as complete.","proposed_test_case":"Send 20+ sequential authenticated requests to the dashboard endpoint as an analyst-role user and verify all return 200. Optionally, pin requests to each backend server individually (bypassing the load balancer) and confirm each one returns 200 for the analyst role.","information_gaps":["Exact number of backend servers and load balancer configuration","Whether the issue is in application-level RBAC config, a database migration, or a cached policy file","Whether non-analyst roles on the same servers work correctly (confirmed anecdotally but not verified systematically)","Server-side access logs correlating 403 responses to specific backend instances"]}
